query: phospholemman membrane protein location and function

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Membrane -- Cellular Level -- Membrane Composition
retrieved_docs.content: At the basic level, the cell membrane is a collection of lipids (namely phospholipid) and protein components. The phospholipid structure forms from a hydrophilic phosphorylated glycerol "head" and two hydrophobic fatty acid "tails." The cell membrane possesses a phospholipid bilayer, with the hydrophilic heads facing outwards and the hydrophobic tails facing inwards. [1] An additional lipid component is cholesterol, which prevents excessive membrane fluidity at elevated temperatures and ironically allows for membrane fluidity at decreased temperatures by preventing lipids from packing together. [2] The protein component of the cell membrane includes either integral proteins (embedded within the membrane) and peripheral proteins (outside of the membrane).  Examples of essential proteins include any membrane channels, membrane pores, membrane receptors, and adhesion anchors. [3] Peripheral proteins loosely attach to the outside of the cell membrane by electrostatic forces; these peripheral proteins serve multiple functions in the cell membrane, including signaling, recognition, membrane trafficking, cell division, and cell structure. [4] The lipid component (phospholipids, cholesterol, and glycolipids) allows for the high permeability of lipid-soluble compounds such as steroid hormones, carbon dioxide, and oxygen. These lipids also inhibit the passive diffusion of hydrophilic substances. The protein component is responsible for transporting hydrophilic substances such as water, glucose, and ions.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cellular receptors can be either intracellular or cell surface proteins. Intracellular receptors that located in the cytoplasm or nucleus of target cells bind lipid-soluble chemical messengers. Cell surface receptors are transmembrane proteins that bind water-soluble chemical messengers.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Both PMCA and SERCA are P-type ATPases. All P-type ATPases exhibit a similar structure and mechanism of action. Both of these pumps use the energy derived from ATP to pump calcium against their concentration gradients.) [14] The PMCA and SERCA pump both contain a 10-transmembrane spanning domain called the "M" (membrane) domain. Also, they contain several cytosolic domains, including an "A" (actuator) domain, an "N" (nucleotide-binding) domain, and a "P" (Phosphorylation) domain. [15] The amino carboxy-terminal of these peptides both face the cytosolic compartment. [15] [16] [9] The active site of the PMCA pump is located between membrane segments 4 and 5, which forms a cytosolic loop and contains the ATP-binding and phosphorylation site. The PMCA pump expresses in all tissues and cell types. It exhibits a 1:1 Ca/ATP stoichiometry. [17] [9] [18] In contrast, the SERCA pump has a 2:1 Ca/ATP stoichiometry. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The beta subunit can be found in both the Cav1 (L-type) and Cav2 (P/Q, N, and R-type) families of voltage-gated calcium channels. [19] [20] [23] It influences both membrane trafficking and the expression of voltage-gated calcium channels. [19] [20] [23] The beta subunit is localized on the cytoplasmic side of the membrane at the intracellular surface and interacts with domains I and II of the a1 subunit. [21] [19] Like the beta subunit, the a2d subunit contributes to membrane trafficking and overall expression of the Cav1 and Cav2 classes of VGCCs. [20] [19] [23] The beta and a2d subunits appear to act synergistically in this function [20] . This subunit is composed of an a2 subunit and delta subunit linked by a disulfide bond. [20] It is located on the extracellular side of the membrane and contains a single transmembrane domain. [12] [24] The a2d subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels can be found in both the Cav1 (L-type) and Cav2 (P/Q, N, and R-type) families of voltage-gated calcium channels. [19] The gamma subunit is a membrane-bound protein containing four transmembrane segments. [19] This subunit is thought to influence VGCC channel function and gating. It also regulates membrane trafficking and interacts with the voltage-sensor of the a1 subunit via a linker segment. [24] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Cellular Level -- Cell-Surface Receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Cell-surface receptors are transmembrane proteins embedded in the plasma membrane of target cells. These receptors consist of an extracellular domain containing the ligand-binding site, a transmembrane domain, and an intracellular domain that transmits the signal inside the cell. When a water-soluble ligand binds to a cell-surface receptor, it activates the receptor, initiating a signal transduction pathway that alters cellular function. The 3 types of cell-surface receptors include G protein–coupled receptors (GPCRs), ion channel receptors, and enzyme-linked receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Cellular Level -- Cell-Surface Receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Additional types of receptors with intrinsic enzymatic activity include protein tyrosine phosphatases, which remove phosphate from phosphotyrosine residues; protein-serine/threonine kinases, which add phosphate on serine or threonine residues; and guanylyl cyclases, which catalyze the formation of cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP).

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Cellular Level -- Transportation
retrieved_docs.content: This regulated secretory pathway only takes place in cells specialized for secretion. (eg, B lymphocytes secrete antibodies). Vesicles containing specialized proteins for secretion bud off of the trans-Golgi network region of the Golgi apparatus. The secretory vesicle then remains in the cytosol. The ligand binds to a ligand-binding site on the extracellular matrix facing transmembrane receptor protein. After binding to a ligand (eg, neurotransmitter or hormone), the receptor undergoes a conformational change, thus activating the signal transduction pathway. The signal transduction pathway could cause an increase in intracellular calcium levels. The extracellular concentration of calcium is 1x10 -3 . Intracellular calcium concentration is 1x10 -7 . Calcium influx activates the cell once intracellular concentration reaches 5x10 -6 M. The cytosolic secretory vesicles then move to the plasma membrane for exocytosis. [6] Peptide Bonds

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: IP3 channels are consist of four subunits that make up a tetrameric channel embedded in the membrane of the endoplasmic and sarcoplasmic reticulum. [29] The IP3R monomers have a binding pocket, a regulatory domain in the cytosol, and a pore region. The pore region is composed of a six-transmembrane spanning helix located near the C-terminus through which calcium flows. The ligand-binding pocket is located near the N-terminal and binds to IP3. [10] An essential aspect of these channels is the various scaffolding and cytoskeletal proteins it associates with to form macromolecular complexes with other receptors and provide stability. Furthermore, various adaptor proteins link the IP3 receptor to different other molecules in the cell. [30] Finally, various calcium-binding proteins also have binding sites on the channel to modulate their function. [30] Its ligand-binding site for inositol triphosphate (IP3) is located near the N-terminal of the protein. [31] The ligand-binding site must be occupied on all four monomers by IP3 to induce the necessary conformational change that sensitizes the channel to calcium. [29] Once the channel undergoes this conformational change and calcium has bound to its binding site, the channel opens. Calcium then passes through from the ER or SR to the cytoplasm. This sequence of events often follows the activation of another receptor, such as a G-protein-coupled receptor or receptor tyrosine kinase on the plasma membrane to which the IP3 receptor couples. These cell surface receptors activate an intracellular signaling pathway that leads to the activation of phospholipase C (PLC), which produces IP3 as one of its end-products.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Messengers -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The GPCR is the largest class of membrane coupled receptors, composed of a seven-transmembrane segment and a heterotrimeric G protein. Ligands of GPCR can be light, ions, or complex proteins. [9] The ligand-binding site of GPCRs is located on extracellular surface membranes. The intracellular surface interacts with heterotrimeric G proteins. G proteins have three subunits (alpha, beta, and gamma). When ligand interacts with specific GPCR, heterotrimeric G protein dissociates into alpha subunit and beta-gamma dimer. The alpha subunit is classified into three categories based on its function: G s , G i ,  and G q . The Gs alpha subunit leads to the stimulation of the enzyme adenylyl cyclase. Adenylyl cyclase converts ATP to cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP). The cAMP is a second messenger that activates protein kinase A and also interacts with other effector molecules. The Gi alpha subunit inhibits adenylyl cyclase. It results in cAMP depletion due to the tonic phosphodiesterase activity. The G q alpha subunit activates the phospholipase C (PLC). PLC cleaves phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) into inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3) and diacylglycerol (DAG). These molecules act as second messengers. IP3 causes calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum, and DAG activates protein kinase C. Protein kinase C can phosphorylate many other proteins. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Oxidative Phosphorylation -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The mitochondrion consists of inner and outer membranes, both of which are composed of phospholipid bilayers and integral membrane proteins involved in enzymatic action and molecular transport. The inner membrane has inward-facing fold-like projections known as cristae that vastly increase the surface area of the membrane to maximize the amount of energy production. The protein complexes involved in the electron transport chain are studded along this membrane. The inner membrane envelops the matrix, which houses mitochondrial DNA, ribosomes, and a multitude of enzymes and metabolites. The space between the inner and outer membrane is known as the intermembrane space; this is the site of hydrogen ion deposition for the protein complexes in the electron transport chain. The increased hydrogen ion (H+ ion) concentration (and effectual decreased pH) generate a membrane potential across the inner mitochondrial membrane. [2] [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Pathophysiology -- Altered Trafficking
retrieved_docs.content: The proper functioning of cellular receptors requires them to be appropriately located within the cell, which is accomplished by cellular trafficking. For example, cell surface receptors must be transported from the rough endoplasmic reticulum to the Golgi apparatus and then to the membrane of a secretory vesicle, which subsequently fuses with the plasma membrane to insert the receptor. Defective trafficking can lead to reduced receptor numbers on the plasma membrane. This can result from defects in the cellular receptor itself, such as mutations or inappropriate posttranslational modifications; defects in the cellular trafficking machinery, including mutations in trafficking proteins or disruption of the cytoskeleton; disruption of protein-protein interactions, either between the receptor and chaperone proteins or with other proteins necessary for proper trafficking; environmental factors like nutrient availability; and infectious agents, such as viruses that hijack the cell's trafficking machinery.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Oxidative Phosphorylation -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The ETC shuttle electrons from one complex to the next, utilizing hydrophilic electron carriers, such as cytochrome C (that can travel freely through the intermembrane space) and hydrophobic, such as coenzyme Q10 (that can diffuse through the phospholipid bilayer of the inner membrane). The genetic information for the four complexes and the free electron carriers gets housed in both mitochondrial DNA (mDNA) and nuclear DNA.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: When calcium concentrations are low in the cytosol, the PMCA pump is in the resting position, elicited by the blocking of the phosphorylation site by the C-terminal auto-inhibitory domain. A calcium-sensing protein called cAM binds to calcium when concentrations of cytosolic calcium increase. This causes a conformational change driven by the interaction of Ca-cAM and the autoinhibitory site, which dissociates from the active site. Calcium is transported to the extracellular environment when it ATP-phosphorylates a conserved aspartate residue. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The variety of different calcium channels in myocardial cells contribute to different physiological activities associated with cardiovascular functioning, and mutations in these channels contribute to various diseases of the cardiovascular system. For example, the SERCA pump plays a vital role in mediating the positively chronotropic effects of beta-adrenergic stimulation because beta-adrenergic generates cAMP, which activates a protein kinase that phosphorylates phospholamban. When phospholamban is phosphorylated, it disinhibits the SERCA pump, increasing the rate of cytosolic uptake of calcium into the sarcoplasmic reticulum and decreasing the relaxation time of the heart during increased physical exertion. Mutations in the SERCA pump have been linked to dilated cardiomyopathy. [49] [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Noradrenergic Synapse -- Mechanism -- Receptor Binding
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha-1 receptors further subdivide into alpha-1a, alpha-1b, and alpha-1d receptors. These receptors are located postsynaptically in regions of the brain including the locus coeruleus, olfactory bulb, cerebral cortex, dentate gyrus, amygdala, and thalamus. Alph-1 receptors have intermediate binding affinity to norepinephrine and couple to the Gq protein signaling pathway. In this pathway, phospholipase C (PLC) is activated to convert phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) to inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3) and diacylglycerol (DAG) on the cell membrane. IP3 is released to the cytosol and binds to transmembrane IP3 receptors located on the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) which functions as a calcium channel. When bound, the receptor undergoes a conformational change leading to the release of calcium from the ER to the cytosol. DAG remains in the cell membrane and positively regulates protein kinase C (PKC), which functions to phosphorylate other proteins. These combined effects produce excitatory cellular effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, C Peptide -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: C-peptide is a 31-amino acid polypeptide and is negatively charged. In mammals, the 8 residues (positions 1, 3, 6, 11, 12, 21, 27, and 31) are conserved, and the C-terminal pentapeptide has been shown to interact with the cell membrane and elicit signaling pathways. [3] Although the exact mechanism of binding is not known, the binding characteristics and the intracellular effects could be modified by pertussis toxin, suggesting that the G-protein coupled receptors might be involved. [4] The binding of C-peptide was shown to elevate the intracellular calcium levels. It also can induce phospholipase C, protein kinase C isoforms, Rho A,  and p38 MAPK in renal tubular cells and fibroblasts. [5] Activation of the PI3 kinase, Akt, and PPAR-gamma is also observed in fibroblasts, myoblasts, renal tubular cells, and lymphocytes. [6] In endothelial cells, C-peptide was shown to induce nitric oxide release by enhancing the expression of eNOS mRNA and protein in aortic endothelial cells. It was also shown to stimulate Na, K-ATPase in renal tubular cells in vitro. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2773
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropetin and glukagon are both members of the broad class “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein.” As peptide hormones, they share the structural characteristic of being protein‑based signaling molecules, yet they serve very different physiological functions. Erythropetin regulates erythropoiesis by stimulating the production of red blood cells, while glukagon raises blood glucose levels by promoting glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Recognizing that these distinct hormones belong to the same molecular category highlights their common biochemical nature and informs both basic understanding of hormone biology and the development of protein‑based therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon (ACTH) and glukagon are both peptide hormones that belong to the broader category of amino acids, peptides, or proteins. Because they are members of this biochemical class, a deficiency (“nedostatek”) can be appropriately applied as a qualifier to describe clinical states of insufficient ACTH or glukagon. This reflects that both hormones share structural characteristics as protein‑based signaling molecules and that their lack can be recognized and described in medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2778
retrieved_docs.content: that can disrupt cellular membranes and provoke immune reactions, while human insulin is the principal hormone governing carbohydrate metabolism and energy storage. Toxoids, which are inactivated toxins, are used to safely stimulate protective immune physiology without causing disease. Together, these diverse organisms, molecules, anatomical structures, and pharmacologic agents illustrate the wide range of factors that influence human physiological processes and underscore their importance in clinical assessment, diagnosis, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1500
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is a hormone and calcium‑regulating substance that belongs to the group of parathyroid hormones and analogues. It functions within the broader category of hormones and agents that control calcium metabolism and is directly related to the hormone calcitonin, highlighting the interplay between these two regulators of calcium homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Glukagon is classified as a hormone and, more specifically, as a peptide hormone; consequently, it belongs to the broader biochemical category of amino acids, peptides, or proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1442
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein is a broad biochemical category that encompasses both erythropoetin and glukagon, indicating that these two substances are proteinaceous molecules rather than simple small‑molecule drugs. Erythropoetin is further linked to the parent class proteiny, reinforcing its classification as a member of the protein family. Together, these relationships clarify that erythropoetin and glukagon function as peptide hormones derived from protein structures, a fact that is essential for understanding their roles in physiological processes such as red blood cell production and glucose regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Cinacalcet is classified as an anti‑parathyroid agent, meaning it belongs to the drug class that suppresses parathyroid hormone (PTH) secretion. By activating calcium‑sensing receptors on parathyroid cells, cinacalcet reduces PTH levels, which is particularly useful in managing secondary hyperparathyroidism associated with chronic kidney disease and other disorders of calcium‑phosphate metabolism. Recognizing cinacalcet as an anti‑parathyroid agent guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, monitoring calcium and phosphate balance, and optimizing patient outcomes in conditions where excess PTH is detrimental.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1653
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, a hormone classified under the functional group “hormones by function,” exerts several key physiologic effects. It decreases renal calcium (Ca++) excretion, helping to conserve calcium in the body, and it stimulates fluid and electrolyte absorption in the small intestine, contributing to overall mineral balance. Parathormon’s actions are closely linked to those of kalcitonin, another calcium‑regulating hormone; the two hormones interact in a complementary manner to maintain calcium homeostasis, with kalcitonin generally opposing the calcium‑raising effects of parathormon. Understanding these relationships is essential for managing disorders of calcium metabolism and for guiding therapeutic interventions that target these hormonal pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1754
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormone, which belongs to the class of parathyroid hormones and analogues, exerts several key physiologic effects. It reduces renal calcium excretion, helping to conserve calcium in the body, and it stimulates fluid and electrolyte absorption in the small intestine, contributing to overall mineral balance. Calcitonin is related to parathormone, reflecting the coordinated interaction of these hormones in the regulation of calcium homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1149
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge links a range of microorganisms—Aspergillus fumigatus, Corynebacterium diphtheriae (which also contributes to upper‑respiratory infections), HIV, and Vibrio cholerae—to human physiology as models for infection, immune response and disease mechanisms. Biochemical regulators such as α‑2‑antiplasmin, viral antigens, diiodotyrosine, the basilar artery (arteria basilaris), the calcium‑binding protein calidin, and the enkephalin peptide Ala(2)‑MePhe(4)‑Gly(5) are described as influencing normal and pathological processes. Synthetic prostaglandin analogues dinoproston and dinoprost, classified as prostaglandin oxytocics, are noted for stimulating uterine contractions. Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, particularly estradiol, are identified as hormone‑replacement agents for menopausal disorders and as modulators of fertility. Cardiac‑active glycosides such as strophanthins and natriuretic peptide type B are highlighted for their roles in sodium excretion, cardiovascular homeostasis, and cardiac function. The term “Allowed qualifier” is used as a regulatory descriptor in the physiology of toxoids. Together these entities illustrate the interconnected roles of infectious agents, hormonal and enzymatic regulators, vascular structures, and specialized peptide systems in health, disease, and therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 128

Calcitonin has a physiological relationship with parathormon, as both hormones participate in calcium homeostasis. Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how diverse pharmacologic agents, excipients, therapeutic classes, mechanisms of action, and physiological roles are organized within C [Preparations] and related classification systems, informing formulation design, diagnosis, treatment selection, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1347
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is categorized under the disposition of hormones, indicating its role as a hormonal agent. It is a member of the group termed “parathyroid hormones and analogues,” linking it to other related endocrine substances. One of the key physiologic effects of parathormon is the stimulation of fluid and electrolyte absorption in the small intestine, highlighting its importance in maintaining intestinal electrolyte balance and overall mineral homeostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1378
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxykortikosteron is a steroid compound that is mapped from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, indicating that deoxykortikosteron is derived from or corresponds to tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, thereby highlighting the biochemical relationship between these two hormone-related molecules.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 345
retrieved_docs.content: trisaccharides qualify biosynthetic processes, particularly in the synthesis of gonadotropin glycoproteins; choriogonadotropin and follitropin β belong to the gonadotropin structural class and incorporate specific trisaccharide moieties that affect hormone stability, receptor interaction, and biological activity. Understanding these hierarchical drug classifications, their biosynthetic origins, and the diverse molecular modifiers that regulate biosynthesis aids clinicians in selecting appropriate immunosuppressive therapy and in appreciating the complex mechanisms underlying hormone production and other therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2748
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormone is a broad class of lipid‑soluble signaling molecules, and both kalcifediol and kalcitriol are specific members within this class. As child entities of steroid hormone, kalcifediol and kalcitriol share the fundamental structural features and receptor‑mediated mechanisms characteristic of steroid hormones, linking them to the physiological processes regulated by this hormone family. Recognizing kalcifediol and kalcitriol as steroid hormones clarifies their role in endocrine pathways, such as calcium and phosphate metabolism, and informs clinical approaches to disorders of vitamin D metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2915
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), is classified as a member of the peptidy group, meaning it is a peptide hormone that functions within the body’s endocrine signaling pathways.
